missed_arguments.description: The author argues that the ability to 'guess' a writer's intentions is the cognitive basis for intrinsic assessment and defines the very experience of reading 'poor' writing as the experience of tentative or uncertain guessing.
missed_arguments.source_passage: It is true that our guesses about the aims of a poor writer are often highly tentative and uncertain, which is chiefly why we judge him to be a poor writer. But in many cases we are confident of having correctly guessed the complex aims of the writer.
missed_arguments.suggested_claim: The degree of confidence a reader has in their 'guess' of a writer's intentions is an intuitive indicator of that writer's skill level.

missed_arguments.description: Hirsch provides a specific logical defense for why 'Correctness' must be scored separately: because the pedagogical methods used to teach it (drill/memorization) are fundamentally different from those used to teach the 'art' of presentation.
missed_arguments.source_passage: Moreover, since the teaching of correctness is often a matter of drill and memorization, one may reasonably score it apart from skill of presentation, an art which one teaches very differently.
missed_arguments.suggested_claim: The separation of assessment categories should mirror the separation of pedagogical methods to provide useful feedback for instruction.

missed_arguments.description: The author argues that using the analytical categories identified by Diederich (Ideas, Usage, etc.) as a weighting system is self-defeating because it codifies the very points of disagreement that cause unreliability in the first place.
missed_arguments.source_passage: For, to use the categories about which readers disagree is to codify disagreement and to lose widespread acceptance from the start.
missed_arguments.suggested_claim: Standardizing assessment using the specific traits that readers naturally prioritize will fail because it institutionalizes the underlying conflict between reader types.

distorted_claims.problem: The claim states that assessment must be 'analytical rather than holistic,' but Hirsch later clarifies that he advocates for holistic scoring within specific analytical partitions.
distorted_claims.source_passage: If holistic scoring proves to be adequate for assessing quality of presentation, then holistic scoring in this category recommends itself on theoretical as well as practical grounds.
distorted_claims.suggested_fix: Reliability and validity should be sought by analytically partitioning the assessment into three domains (intentions, presentation, correctness), but using holistic scoring within the presentation domain.

distorted_claims.problem: The claim omits the critical constraint of 'synonymy' which is the lynchpin of Hirsch's experimental method.
distorted_claims.source_passage: the possibility of expertly rewriting most papers, while keeping synonymous the complex intentions of the two versions
distorted_claims.suggested_fix: It is possible to expertly rewrite papers to be more readable while maintaining strict synonymy with the original's complex intentions.

missing_warrants.missing_warrant: The 'difficulty factor' used in athletics is a valid model for writing because both involve a performance of a craft where technical execution can be logically separated from the ambition of the attempt.
The 'Heresy of Paraphrase' is false; it is possible to alter the linguistic form of a message to reduce cognitive load without altering the underlying semantic meaning or 'complex of aims'.

missing_counter_arguments.counter_argument: The 'Aesthetic of Difficulty' suggests that in certain genres or rhetorical situations, 'least effort' is not the goal; rather, a certain level of 'productive friction' is required to engage the reader's critical faculties.
missing_counter_arguments.why_important: This challenges the universality of 'Relative Readability' as the sole intrinsic standard.

missing_counter_arguments.counter_argument: Certified assessors might achieve reliability by simply adopting the researcher's specific aesthetic biases rather than discovering a 'normative' or 'correct' score.
missing_counter_arguments.why_important: It questions whether the 'correctness' of the score is an objective reality or a successful socialization into the author's own theoretical framework.

granularity_issues.explanation: All three claims essentially argue that the validity of assessment is tied to its consistency with societal judgment (the 'court of last resort').
C1 identifies assessment as the primary 'snag' or obstacle, and C17 explains why: because no current method is both reliable and valid. They are parts of the same overarching conclusion.

overall_assessment.summary: The extraction is exceptionally thorough, capturing even minor nuances of the text's argument. However, it over-simplifies Hirsch's stance on 'analytical' vs 'holistic' methods; Hirsch actually proposes a hybrid (analytical partitioning with holistic execution). It also misses the critical philosophical warrant regarding the 'synonymy of intentions' that allows his experimental rewrite method to function.
